title: Arkansas SB6: "Pleading" When We Should Be Defying
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman critiques Arkansas SB6 (the Unborn Child Protection Act), arguing it merely pleads with the Supreme Court to overturn Roe rather than defying it, and fails to provide equal protection by exempting mothers from prosecution and treating abortion as less than murder.
audiences: State legislator weighing abolition vs. incremental bills
Constitutional originalist or co-equal branches advocate
Pastor or Christian considering Romans 13 vs. defiance

questions.question: What is SB6 being marketed as, and why does the author say the marketing doesn't match reality?
questions.answer: SB6 is being celebrated as a sweeping abolition victory because its text claims to abolish abortion and protect all human life. Silberman warns that pro-life politicians are co-opting abolitionist language while passing legislation that still submits to the Supreme Court and refuses equal protection, twisting the meaning of abolitionism in the process.
questions.quote: It's passage is being celebrated as a pro-life victory, even more so than most bills because SB6 purports to "abolish abortion in Arkansas and protect the lives of unborn children; and to protect all human life."

questions.question: How does SB6's posture toward the Supreme Court differ from the abolition bills introduced in other states?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing abolition vs. incremental bills
questions.answer: Unlike abolition legislation in Oklahoma, Texas, Idaho, Missouri, Indiana, Arizona, Washington, and Alaska, SB6 accepts that the Supreme Court has authority to keep abortion legal in the states. From that submissive posture, its authors merely beg the justices to reverse Roe rather than instructing the state to ignore unconstitutional rulings.
questions.quote: Unlike abolitionist legislation that has been introduced in Oklahoma, Texas, Idaho, Missouri, Indiana, Arizona, Washington, and Alaska, SB6 accepts the authority of the supreme court to force child sacrifice on the states. From this posture of submission, the authors of SB6 "plead with the United States Supreme Court to do the right thing."

questions.question: What evidence does Silberman cite for why pleading with the Supreme Court has not worked?
questions.answer: He invokes Frederick Douglass's maxim that power concedes nothing without a demand and notes that pro-lifers have pleaded with the Court in 38 abortion cases over 48 years, every single one of which left abortion legal. Continued pleading, he says, fits Einstein's definition of insanity.
questions.quote: We've pled with them to do the right thing in 38 abortion-related supreme court cases over the last 48 years. Every single one of these cases resulted in continued legal abortion. The supreme court is not conceding anything.

questions.question: What does an actual abolition bill instruct the state to do, according to the OK SB495 example?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing abolition vs. incremental bills
questions.answer: An abolition bill treats hostile federal rulings, statutes, treaties, and orders as void where they deprive an unborn child of the right to life, and it directs the attorney general and district attorneys to enforce state anti-abortion law regardless of any conflicting federal authority.
questions.quote: "[E]nsure the enforcement of Chapter 24 of Title 21 of the Oklahoma Statutes in relation to abortion regardless of any contrary or conflicting federal statute, regulation, treaty, executive order or court rulings."

questions.question: On what theological and constitutional grounds does Silberman justify defiance of the Supreme Court?
questions.audience: Pastor or Christian considering Romans 13 vs. defiance
questions.answer: He argues defiance is mandated by Scripture (Acts 5:29, Exodus 1:17, Daniel 3:18) whenever rulers command what God forbids or forbid what God commands. It is also required to preserve the Constitution itself, because if a court can simply opine away the right to life, the republic has been replaced by an oligarchy of unaccountable judges.
questions.quote: This defiance is mandated by God's word in cases where governing authorities command that which God forbids or forbid that which God commands (Acts 5:29, Ex 1:17, Dan 3:18). Defiance is also mandated if we are to uphold  the U.S. Constitution. If a court can simply opine away the right to life, then we don't have a Constitutional republic. We have a court of oligarchs with unconditional, uncheckable power.

questions.question: How does Silberman use Thomas Jefferson to refute judicial supremacy?
questions.audience: Constitutional originalist or co-equal branches advocate
questions.answer: He cites Jefferson's argument that the Constitution erected no single tribunal as ultimate arbiter, but instead made the branches co-equal and co-sovereign. When one branch oversteps, the others are obligated to check it rather than plead with it.
questions.quote: "The Constitution has erected no such single tribunal, knowing that to whatever hands confided, with the corruptions of time and party, its members would become despots. It has more wisely made all the departments co-equal and co-sovereign within themselves."

questions.question: How does SB6 fail to provide equal protection to preborn children?
questions.answer: SB6 treats killing a preborn child as merely a Class C felony for the abortionist, capped at a $100,000 fine and ten years in prison, while explicitly forbidding any criminal charge against the mother who procured the abortion. That treats the preborn child as less than fully human under the law.
questions.quote: The abortionist is subject to "a fine not to exceed one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000) or imprisonment not to exceed ten (10) years, or both," and the bill does not authorize "the charging or conviction of a woman with any criminal offense in the death of her own unborn child."

questions.question: Why does Silberman argue that unequal-protection bills actually undercut the pro-life argument in court?
questions.answer: He points to footnote 54 of Roe v. Wade, where Justice Blackmun observed that Texas's failure to charge mothers as principals or accomplices, and its lighter penalty for abortion than for murder, undermined any claim that the fetus is a person. Bills like SB6 reproduce that very inconsistency and hand the courts a reason to keep abortion legal.
questions.quote: "There are other inconsistencies between Fourteenth Amendment status and the typical abortion statute. It has already been pointed out that in Texas the woman is not a principal or an accomplice with respect to an abortion upon her. If the fetus is a person, why is the woman not a principal or an accomplice? Further, the penalty for criminal abortion specified by Art. 1195 is significantly less than the maximum penalty for murder prescribed by Art. 1257 of the Texas Penal Code. If the fetus is a person, may the penalties be different?"

questions.question: What concrete cost did SB6 impose on the abolition movement in Arkansas?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing abolition vs. incremental bills
questions.answer: Arkansas abolitionists had been working with a legislator who was poised to file a true Abolition of Abortion in Arkansas Act. When Rapert announced SB6 in November, that legislator concluded the political momentum was already behind Rapert's bill and decided not to introduce the abolition bill, so SB6 functioned as a substitute that displaced an actual abolition effort.
questions.quote: SB6 is especially disappointing because Arkansas abolitionists had been meeting with legislators and one in particular was on the verge of putting forward a bill of abolition. But when Rapert's bill was announced in November, the legislator who was considering putting forward an actual abolition bill thought that all the momentum inside the capitol was behind Rapert's bill and decided not to put forward the Abolition of Abortion in Arkansas Act.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Silberman explicitly links to this article when explaining why bills like SB6 are opposed for serving as substitutes for true abolition.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Develops the same critique of pro-life submission to the Supreme Court that SB6 exemplifies.

related.title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
related.reason: Direct treatment of the pleading-versus-defiance posture toward the Court that SB6 adopts.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: Defends the constitutional and historical legitimacy of state nullification, the very mechanism SB6 refuses to use.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Counters the same defeatism about defying the Court that animates SB6's pleading posture.

related.title: Anti-SB13 Campaigners Aren't Ignorant. They're Trying to Deceive You.
related.reason: Parallel case study of pro-life opposition to a true abolition bill (Oklahoma SB13), illuminating the tactics also at work around SB6.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: Lays out the equal-protection principle that SB6 violates by exempting mothers and reducing the penalty for child-killing.
